Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Plaintiff Azar Afsharipour For Attorneys Fees And Costs Pursuant To Government Code 12965(B)
Off Calendar Apr-01-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. LAWRENCE A. ORGAN, ESQ. of Law Offices of Lawrence A. Organ (Tel No. 415-453-4740) and atty. BRYAN K. SCHWARTZ of Bryan Schwartz Law (Tel No. 510-444-9300) appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. LINDA MILLER SAVITT, ESQ. of Ballard Rosenberg Golper & Savitt, LLP, Tel No. 818-508-3700 appearing for Defendant Saks & Company.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF AZAR AFSHARIPOUR'S motion for attorney's fees is granted in part. Plaintiff is awarded $607,601 in attorney's fees, including her counsel's work on this motion and the motion to tax costs. Plaintiff's proffered lodestar of $844,215 is far too high for a relatively simple single plaintiff employment harassment case that was settled a month and half before trial and did not involve any particularly novel or difficult legal issues or an excessive number of motions and the only difficult factual issue was plaintiff's lack of economic damages. Moreover, while there were 20 depositions, many of them were not lengthy, and there was minimal expert work. Based on all the papers filed on this motion, a review of the court file and the court's knowledge of the Bay Area legal marketplace and its experience in handling cases like this one, using plaintiff's proffered lodestar as the starting point, the court has constructed a reasonable lodestar. The reasonable lodestar has been enhanced by a 1.25 multiplier due to the excellent result achieved and the contingent risk. The reasonable lodestar amount of $460,481 was arrived at by: 1) starting with plaintiff's adjusted lodestar on page 11 of her reply brief of $844,215; 2) subtracting $45,515 attributable to the work on the moving papers for this motion; 3) subtracting $9548 attributable to administrative work which is non-compensable overhead; 4) subtracting $48,641 due to the determination that the reasonable rate for Mr. Organ and Mr. Schwartz is $500 per hour; 5) subtracting $164,910 due to the determination that the reasonable rate for the associates, law clerks and paralegals was 40% less than the rates ascribed to them; 6) subtracting $115,120 due to the determination that the hours billed were 20% more than reasonable for this case, there was no need for two primary or lead attorneys and there was considerable duplication of effort. The 1.25 multiplier brings that amount to $575,601. Based on the determination that a reasonable amount for the work done on this motion and the costs motion is $32,000, calculated at 80 hours at a blended rate of $400 per hour, the total amount of fees awarded is $607,601. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jul-29-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. BRYAN J. SCHWARTZ, TEL. NO. 1-510-444-9300 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. LAWRENCE A. ORGAN OF THE CA CIVIL RIGHTS LAW GROUP, TEL. NO. 1-510-978-4880 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. LINDA MILLER SAVITT OF BRGS LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-818-508-3700 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT SAKS & COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication - The motion for summary judgment is denied and the motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. The motion is denied as to issues 1-3. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding the existence of the age and sex harassment and defendant's knowledge of the conduct that supports the claims. Whether the conduct was sufficiently severe or pervasive to constitute harassment is a factual issue. See Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2006) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 264. Plaintiff's and Ms. Wald's declarations show that Mr. Nikolaev routinely verbally and physically assaulted both of them and plaintiff witnessed some of the mistreatment of Ms. Wald. The court grants summary adjudication as to issue 4. Plaintiff fails to present evidence of an adverse employment action that supports the retaliation claim. The single formal discipline of plaintiff and the litigation hold memorandum are insufficient as a matter of law to constitute an adverse employment action. Those acts did not materially alter the terms or conditions of plaintiff's employment. In Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1060, the employer engaged in a pattern of systematic retaliation. The court denies summary adjudication as to issue 5. Plaintiff's and Ms. Wald's declarations show that defendant had sufficient notice of the misconduct and it did not respond in an appropriate manner to support the failure to prevent cause of action. The court denies summary adjudication as to issue 6. Defendant failed to maintain its burden of production. At most, defendant has shown that Mr. Hagman was not a managing agent. Defendant fails, however, to demonstrate that any of the other employees of defendant that had notice of Mr. Nikolaev's conduct and/or had a role in his discipline were not managing agents. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/eg

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-20-2014 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: WILLIAM JHAVERI-WEEKS, 510.444.9300, FOR PLAINTIFF AZAR AFSHARIPOUR.
STEPHANIE CANTOR, ESQ., 818.508.3700, FOR DEFENDANT SAKS & COMPANY.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling re: DEFENDANT SAKS & COMPANY's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The demurrer to the fifth cause of action for negligent retention is sustained without leave to amend. This cause of action is preempted by the worker's compensation exclusivity provision. (Lab. Code sec. 3600(a).) There is a "tripartite system for classifying injuries arising in the course of employment" for purposes of determining whether that injury is covered by the exclusive remedy provisions of the workers' compensation law. (Fermino v. FedCo, Inc. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 701, 713.) "First, there are injuries caused by employer negligence or without employer fault that are compensated at the normal rate under the workers' compensation system. Second, there are injuries caused by ordinary employer conduct that intentionally, knowingly or recklessly harms an employee, for which the employee may be entitled to extra compensation under section 4553. Third, there are certain types of intentional employer conduct which bring the employer beyond the boundaries of the compensation bargain, for which a civil action may be brought." (Id. at 714.) The negligent retention cause of action arises from employer negligence, thus falling under the first prong. (See Coit Drapery Cleaners Inc. v. Sequoia Ins. Co. (1993) 141 Cal.App.4th 1595, 1606, holding that a negligent supervision claim would "be barred'by the workers' compensation laws, since [an employee] may not sue for its allegedly negligent or improper supervision.") The authorities cited by Plaintiff do not mandate a different result. Unpublished Federal District Court opinions are not binding. Further, none of the California cases cited discuss preemption of negligent supervision or retention causes of action. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Joyce Finley #8620.
